title: DORA Compliance: What Your SOC Must Deliver
meta_title: DORA Compliance SOC Requirements | Nordic SOC
meta_description: What DORA requires of your SOC: ICT risk management, incident classification and reporting timelines, testing, third-party risk and audit-ready evidence.
excerpt: DORA applies from 17 January 2025. Here is what the Digital Operational Resilience Act requires of your security operations, and how a modern AI SOC delivers audit-ready detection, classification, reporting and evidence.
author: Nordic SOC
hero_image_alt: DORA compliance and SOC operational resilience for EU financial entities

faq.a: DORA, the Digital Operational Resilience Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2554), applies from 17 January 2025. It is a directly applicable EU regulation, meaning financial entities must comply without waiting for national transposition.

DORA covers a broad range of financial entities, including banks, insurers, investment firms, payment institutions and crypto-asset service providers, as well as the critical ICT third-party providers that supply services to them.

faq.q: What must a SOC deliver for DORA incident reporting?
faq.a: A SOC must consistently detect and classify incidents against DORA criteria and produce initial, intermediate and final reports to the competent authority within regulator-defined timelines, supported by a complete, time-stamped incident timeline.

faq.q: How does an AI SOC help with DORA compliance?
faq.a: An AI SOC provides continuous detection, consistent classification and automatically generated, audit-ready evidence. This reduces reporting latency and ensures the trail DORA expects is captured as events unfold rather than reconstructed afterwards.

faq.q: How does DORA relate to NIS2?
faq.a: NIS2 is a broad cross-sector cybersecurity directive, while DORA is the sector-specific, directly applicable regulation for financial services. Entities subject to both benefit from aligning incident classification and reporting processes across the two frameworks.
